title: Demographic profile and clinical characteristics of Fuchs' endothelial corneal dystrophy in a tertiary eye care center.
abstract: This study was performed to determine the demographic profile and clinical characteristics in patients with Fuchs' endothelial corneal dystrophy (FECD) reporting to a tertiary eye care center in India. It is a retrospective, single-center, observational study. The study included 280 patients (559 eyes) diagnosed with FECD presenting between January 2013 and December 2020. The data was collected from the electronic medical record system of the institute. Patient data included demographic features, clinical characteristics, investigations, and surgical interventions. The mean age of the patients was 62 years. Late-onset FECD (95.7%) was more common than early-onset FECD (4.3%). Male: female ratio for late-onset FECD and early-onset FECD was 1:1.65 and 3:1, respectively. More than one-third of the patients had associated systemic history. Preexisting ocular diseases were seen in 5.9% of eyes. Blurring of vision was seen in 383 eyes (68.5%), 13 eyes (2.1%) had glare, and 163 eyes (29.2%) were asymptomatic. A total of 113 surgical interventions were done in 108 eyes (including repeat transplants). Only cataract surgery was done in 40 (7.2%) eyes, whereas penetrating keratoplasty, Descemet stripping endothelial keratoplasty, and Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty without or with cataract surgery (sequential or triple procedure) were done in 12 (2.1%), 47 (8.4%), and 14 (2.5%) eyes, respectively. Patients with FECD present mostly during the sixth decade. Posterior lamellar keratoplasty is the most common transplant procedure being performed on FECD patients.
full_text:
Demographic profile and clinical characteristics of Fuchs’ endothelial corneal dystrophy in a tertiary eye care center

Purpose:

This study was performed to determine the demographic profile and clinical characteristics in patients with Fuchs’ endothelial corneal dystrophy (FECD) reporting to a tertiary eye care center in India. It is a retrospective, single-center, observational study.

Methods:

The study included 280 patients (559 eyes) diagnosed with FECD presenting between January 2013 and December 2020. The data was collected from the electronic medical record system of the institute. Patient data included demographic features, clinical characteristics, investigations, and surgical interventions.

Results:

The mean age of the patients was 62 years. Late-onset FECD (95.7%) was more common than early-onset FECD (4.3%). Male: female ratio for late-onset FECD and early-onset FECD was 1:1.65 and 3:1, respectively. More than one-third of the patients had associated systemic history. Preexisting ocular diseases were seen in 5.9% of eyes. Blurring of vision was seen in 383 eyes (68.5%), 13 eyes (2.1%) had glare, and 163 eyes (29.2%) were asymptomatic. A total of 113 surgical interventions were done in 108 eyes (including repeat transplants). Only cataract surgery was done in 40 (7.2%) eyes, whereas penetrating keratoplasty, Descemet stripping endothelial keratoplasty, and Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty without or with cataract surgery (sequential or triple procedure) were done in 12 (2.1%), 47 (8.4%), and 14 (2.5%) eyes, respectively.

Conclusion:

Patients with FECD present mostly during the sixth decade. Posterior lamellar keratoplasty is the most common transplant procedure being performed on FECD patients.

Fuchs’ endothelial corneal dystrophy (FECD) is the most common cause of corneal transplantation worldwide. According to a global survey conducted in 116 countries in 2012, about 39% of corneal transplants were performed for FECD alone. It was more common in the USA, Russia, Denmark, and the UK. In the USA, 4% cases had corneal guttata; but due to less coexistence of corneal edema, many patients were not diagnosed with FECD. The prevalence of FECD was 11% in a study done on patients above 50 years of age by Eghrari et al. in Tangier’s Island.

FECD is a bilateral but asymmetric and slowly progressive disorder. It causes rapid endothelial cell loss and changes in the Descemet membrane with focal excrescences called guttae. Also, there is an accumulation of extracellular matrix. Failure of endothelial pump leak function will lead to stromal or epithelial edema, thus showing symptoms of decreased vision, pain, glare, and halos.

Several studies have been conducted on FECD in India, but they have mostly focused on management of the disease. Epidemiological data regarding the profile of the disease over a period, which can be used to determine the impact of FECD in the general population, is scarce. Thus, the purpose of the study was to determine the demographic profile and clinical characteristics in patients with FECD reporting to a tertiary eye care center in India.

Methods

This was a retrospective study conducted on 559 eyes of 280 patients diagnosed with FECD at a tertiary eye care center in eastern India from 2013 to 2020. The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board. Data from patients with a confirmed diagnosis of FECD were collected and reviewed from the electronic medical records of the institute. Patient data included age at presentation, gender, family history, preexisting systemic and ocular disease, and history of past surgical intervention (cataract or keratoplasty). Further, the following data were collected at presentation: duration of the onset of symptoms, uncorrected visual acuity (UCVA), and best corrected visual acuity (BCVA), and whether the patient was asymptomatic or had symptoms of blurring of vision and glare. The Snellen visual acuity was converted into the logarithm of the minimum angle of resolution (logMAR) units for analysis.

Slit-lamp biomicroscopy, status of cornea, status of lens, specular microscopy, and ultrasound pachymetry readings were noted both at presentation and at the last visit. Slit-lamp biomicroscopy examination was done using Zeiss biomicroscope, model 900. Standard spectral illumination method was used to scan the endothelium, and the status of cornea was noted. The status of the lens was noted to observe whether it was clear, cataractous, or pseudophakic. Specular microscopy (Konan, “Noncon ROBO Series NSP-9900”, Japan) was done to evaluate endothelial cell density (ECD), percentage of hexagonal cells (Hex), and coefficient of variation (CV). All patients were diagnosed on the basis of symptoms and visualization of guttae on slit-lamp and specular microscopy.

Surgical interventions involving cataract surgery, endothelial or penetrating keratoplasty (PK) were recorded [Fig. 1]. Choice of type of surgery depended on corneal clarity, pachymetry, and preference of the surgeon. The status of the graft following surgery was noted at the last visit. Also, BCVA at the first postoperative day was noted. Both UCVA and BCVA were noted at the last visit.

Diffuse slit-lamp photographs of patients who underwent keratoplasty. (a, c, e) Three cases of Fuchs’ endothelial corneal dystrophy: preoperative pictures. (b) Two years post penetrating keratoplasty (case a). (d) Two years post Descemet stripping endothelial keratoplasty (case c). (f) One year post Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (case e)

Descriptive and inferential statistical analyses were carried out. Continuous data were presented as mean ± standard deviation (SD) and categorical data as number (%). Comparison between early- and late-onset disease was done with the help of independent t test, Fisher’s exact test, and Chi-square test. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) and Chi-square tests were used to compare the surgical outcomes. The statistical software IBM Statistical Package for the Social Sciences (SPSS) statistics 20.0 (IBM Corporation, Armonk, NY, USA) was used for the analyses of the data.

Results

Demography

The study consisted of 280 patients (559 eyes) diagnosed with FECD having a mean age of 62 (±11.3) years with a range of 26–90 years. FECD contributed to 43.28% of total corneal dystrophies presenting to our institute in the study duration. The baseline characteristics have been described in Table 1.

Baseline characteristics at presentation

Previous surgical interventions

Surgical intervention was done in 111/559 (19.9%) eyes elsewhere before presentation at our institute. Out of 111 eyes, majority had undergone cataract surgery (n = 94, 84.7%), followed by PK (n = 9, 8.1%), Descemet stripping endothelial keratoplasty (DSEK; n = 5, 4.5%), evisceration with implant (n = 1, 0.9%), dacryocystorhinostomy (n = 1, 0.9%), and anterior stromal puncture (n = 1, 0.9%). The mean age of the patients without previous surgical interventions was significantly lower than those with previous surgeries (P < 0.00001).

Clinical features at presentation

The median duration of symptoms at the time of presentation was 12 months (range: 3 days–20 years). At presentation (n = 559), 383 eyes (68.5%) had blurring of vision, 13 eyes (2.3%) had glare, and 163 eyes (29.2%) were asymptomatic. Cornea was clear in 390 (69.8%) eyes, scarring was found in 26 (4.7%) eyes, spheroidal degeneration in two (0.4%) eyes, infiltrate in one (0.2%) eye, and stromal edema was found in 127 (22.7%) eyes. Decompensated cornea was noted in nine (1.6%) eyes at presentation. Lens was clear in 188 (33.6%) eyes, cataract was seen in 255 (45.6%) eyes, pseudophakia in 115 (20.6%) eyes, and aphakia in one (0.2%) eye at the time of presentation. Visual acuity of hand movements (HM) was seen in 10 eyes, perception of light (PL) was seen in 14 eyes, and no perception of light (NPL) was seen in one eye. It was not possible to get specular readings in 79 (14.1%) eyes due to advanced corneal edema.

Surgical intervention

Out of the total number of transplants done at our center in the 8-year study duration, FECD accounted for 3.8% of the transplants. A total of 113 surgical interventions were done in 108 eyes (including repeat transplants) [Fig. 2]. Only cataract surgery was done in 40/559 (7.2%) eyes, whereas PK, DSEK, and Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty (DMEK) without or with cataract surgery (sequential or triple procedure, respectively) were done in 12/559 (2.1%), 47/559 (8.4%), and 14/559 (2.5%) eyes, respectively. Triple procedures were done in 31/559 (5.5%) eyes. Corneal transplantation was required in 5/559 (0.9%) eyes after cataract surgery. Repeat grafts were needed in 5/559 (0.9%) eyes. Bilateral grafts were done in 8/280 (2.9%) patients. Unilateral grafts were done in 52 eyes. Other eye status of these unilateral surgeries on the last visit was clear cornea in 47 eyes, stromal edema in three eyes and preexisting scar in two eyes.

Surgical interventions. Triple procedure: keratoplasty + cataract surgery + intraocular lens implantation. DMEK = Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty, DSEK = Descemet stripping endothelial keratoplasty, PK = penetrating keratoplasty

Clinical outcome

At the last visit, 119 patients (237 eyes) were lost to follow-up and one eye was eviscerated. Among the remaining 321 eyes, cornea was clear in 254 (79.1%) eyes, while scarring, stromal edema, and Descemet membrane folds were observed in nine (2.8%), 52 (16.2%), and 28 (8.7%) eyes, respectively. Lens was clear in 83 (25.9%) eyes and cataractous in 81 (25.2%) eyes. Pseudophakia and aphakia were seen in 192 (59.8%) eyes and one (0.3%) eye, respectively. Four patients followed up from 2013 to 2019. Two patients had no progression in either eye over 7 years. Two patients had DSEK in one eye and the graft was clear at the last visit, and the other eye had no progression. BCVA at the last visit was 0.40 ± 0.47 logMAR units. Visual acuity was HM in six eyes, PL with accurate projection of rays in 12 eyes, and NPL in two eyes.

Surgical outcome

The mean duration from presentation to surgery was 6.1 months in eyes that had undergone surgery. Mean postoperative follow-up after transplant was 22.1 months, ranging from 9 days to 12.5 years (median: 14 months). Surgical outcomes are presented in Table 2. The difference in the postoperative UCVA at the final follow-up visit between PK, DSEK, and DMEK groups was statistically significant (P = 0.016). On comparing two groups individually, the UCVA between PK versus DMEK groups (P = 0.028) and DSEK versus DMEK groups (P = 0.031) was statistically significant, whereas that between PK versus DSEK group was not statistically significant (P = 0.99). The difference in BCVA at the final follow-up visit between PK, DSEK, and DMEK groups was not statistically significant (P = 0.074). Graft rejection was seen in one eye from the PK group, which later developed graft infiltrate, ultimately leading to graft failure. DSEK group showed graft rejection in six eyes, out of which two eyes recovered completely with clear graft and four eyes developed graft failure. DSEK group had primary graft failure in five eyes. DMEK group had primary graft failure in two eyes due to detached scroll. Out of 68 eyes that underwent transplant, 11 (16.2%) eyes developed glaucoma, out of which nine (13.2%) eyes were treated medically, one (1.5%) eye required trabeculectomy, and one (1.5%) eye required Ahmed glaucoma valve. Out of the 40 patients undergoing only cataract surgery, three patients had corneal decompensation at the last visit. The mean duration of follow-up was 19.15 (±20.33) months in 161/280 (57.5%) patients and the rest of the patients were lost to follow-up.

Discussion

FECD is bilateral, asymmetric, and slowly progressive. It is characterized by the formation of endothelial guttae followed by stromal edema leading to decrease in vision and pain. Its most severe form results in blindness.

Early-onset FECD shows changes in the first decade of life, which progresses in the next two to three decades. The early-onset form affects men and women equally and shows an autosomal dominant pattern of inheritance. Late-onset FECD most commonly shows changes in the fifth decade of life, progressing over the next two or three decades. Late-onset form is more commonly seen than the early-onset form. About 95.7% out of 280 patients enrolled in our study belonged to the late-onset form. Most patients are asymptomatic in early stage of the disease in either eye (163/559 eyes, 29.2%), as seen in our study.

Various systemic and ocular diseases are associated with FECD. Almost 35.7% (100/280) patients had associated systemic history in our study. Preexisting ocular diseases were seen in 5.89% eyes, out of which glaucoma was associated in 1.6% eyes. Association of FECD with glaucoma was also noted by Nagarsheth et al. and Pitts and Jay.

We observed in our study that only 19.3% of total eyes required some form of surgical intervention. This indicates that most of the eyes progressed slowly over the study period and could be manged conservatively. The mean duration from presentation to surgery was 6.1 months. Thus, most eyes that required surgery had either advanced FECD at the time of presentation or had decompensated cornea post cataract surgery done before presentation (34.3% eyes). Among 40 eyes that underwent only cataract surgery, only three decompensated till the last visit, thus implying that if adequate precautions are taken during cataract surgery, like using a chondroitin sulfate–based dispersive viscoelastic substance, low-energy and low-flow parameters, damage to endothelium can be minimized in a case of FECD. Some surgeons choose combining keratoplasty with cataract surgery when the central corneal thickness is above 640 μm and the endothelial counts are below 1000 cells/mm2.

The evolution of minimally invasive lamellar endothelial keratoplasty procedures like DSEK and DMEK has resulted in key benefits like faster visual rehabilitation, a tectonically stronger globe, less astigmatism, less infection, and lower rejection rates compared to PK. In our study, while the difference in survival of grafts between PK, DSEK, and DMEK was not statistically significant (because of the low number of transplants overall), a trend toward lesser rejection and failure rate in endothelial keratoplasties can be observed. The optical benefits of endothelial keratoplasty can be observed in our study also, as the difference in UCVA at the last visit was statistically significant between PK and DMEK. Gender distribution, proportion of FECD out of total transplants, types of keratoplasty in cases of FECD, and graft survival were comparable to other studies and have been tabulated in Table 3.

DMEK=Descemet membrane endothelial keratoplasty, DSAEK=Descemet stripping automated endothelial keratoplasty, EK=endothelial keratoplasty, DSEK=Descemet stripping endothelial keratoplasty, F=female, FECD=Fuchs’ endothelial corneal dystrophy, PK=penetrating keratoplasty

Our study has few limitations: we did not evaluate nonsurgical treatment options in patients with FECD, and although smoking is reported as a risk factor for FECD, we did not evaluate it. Since it is a retrospective study and many patients were lost to follow-up, the clinical course of FECD could not be accurately observed. To study the progression of the disease and predict the requirement of surgery, a prospective study would be more informative.

Conclusion

In conclusion, since comprehensive data on the clinical profile and progression of FECD is lacking from the Indian subcontinent, our results add to the existing literature. An overall trend of slow progression of disease and requirement of keratoplasty in less than one-fourth of the study population was observed. We have evolved a long way in the past, where treatment options have changed from PK to DSEK to DMEK. The success of these treatments has benefited patients, as seen in the present study.
